era_context: The Classical Greek world in the early 4th century BCE, in the period before the Battle of Leuctra (371 BCE). Sparta remained the dominant Greek power. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II was stable. In China, the Warring States period was intensifying. Egypt under Nectanebo I was consolidating independence.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Most of Asia beyond Greek contact zones

events.title: Earthquake destroys Helike in Achaea
events.description: According to ancient sources, a catastrophic earthquake destroyed the city of Helike in Achaea in 373 BCE. The earthquake was followed by a tsunami that inundated the coastal city, killing many inhabitants. The city was never rebuilt and was lost to history until its rediscovery by archaeologists in the 20th century. The disaster was so significant that it was remembered and recorded by ancient historians, though the exact date is debated by modern scholars.
events.certainty_note: The destruction of Helike by earthquake and tsunami is attested in multiple ancient sources, but the exact date is uncertain. Ancient sources place it in the early 4th century BCE, and 373 BCE is the traditional date, but some modern scholars propose alternative dates (e.g., 375 BCE or 365 BCE). Archaeological evidence confirms the destruction but does not precisely date it.

events.title: Nectanebo I continues building program in Egypt
events.description: Nectanebo I continued his extensive building program, constructing temples and fortifications throughout Egypt. These projects served both religious and political purposes, reinforcing Egyptian cultural identity and demonstrating the pharaoh's power and piety. The building program was part of a broader cultural renaissance during the 30th Dynasty.
events.certainty_note: Nectanebo I's building activities are well-documented in Egyptian sources and confirmed by archaeological evidence of surviving temples and structures.

disconfirming_evidence: The date of the Helike earthquake is disputed. While 373 BCE is the traditional date found in many ancient sources, modern scholars have proposed alternative dates ranging from 375 BCE to 365 BCE. Some scholars argue that the ancient sources may have confused or conflated multiple earthquakes. The exact relationship between the earthquake and tsunami is also debated—some sources emphasize the earthquake, others the tsunami, and the sequence of events is not entirely clear.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 373 BCE is very sparse. Most surviving accounts focus on major political and military events; natural disasters are recorded only when they had significant political consequences or were remembered in cultural memory. The Helike earthquake is one of the few events from this year that is documented in surviving sources. The sources are heavily weighted toward Greek history; developments in other regions are largely absent. The record is biased toward dramatic events (earthquakes, battles) rather than routine administration, economic activity, or cultural developments.
